Phenomenology focuses on the direct structure of conscious experience as experienced from the first-person point of view.
Dualism asserts that the human mind and physical body are completely distinct, non-identical substances.
Friedrich Nietzsche declared that individuals must create their own values to become the Ubermensch, or overman.
The social contract theory suggests that citizens yield certain freedoms to a government in exchange for social order.
Occam's razor is a problem-solving principle stating that the simplest explanation with the fewest assumptions is usually best.
Aesthetics is the branch of philosophy that examines the nature of beauty, art, and individual taste.
The concept of Dialectical Materialism argues that historical changes are driven by material conditions and class struggles.
Logical positivism claimed that a statement is only meaningful if it is empirically verifiable or logically tautological.
John Locke proposed that the human mind is born as a tabula rasa, which translates to a blank slate.
The myth of Sisyphus was used by Albert Camus to illustrate the human struggle against the inherent absurdity of life.
Pragmatism evaluates the truth of a philosophical theory based on its practical success and real-world application.
The Chinese room argument challenges the notion that computers running software can truly understand language or possess minds.
Deontology is an ethical framework asserting that actions are inherently right or wrong, regardless of their consequences.
Arthur Schopenhauer viewed the universe as driven by a blind, irrational force that he termed the Will.
The concept of Akrasia describes the state of mind where a person acts completely against their own better judgment.
Jean-Jacques Rousseau argued that humans are inherently good but become corrupted by civil society and its institutions.
Determinism contrasts sharply with libertarian free will, which claims humans possess genuine authorship over their choices.
The veil of ignorance is a thought experiment used by John Rawls to design a perfectly fair and just society.
Metaphysics investigates the fundamental nature of reality, including the relationship between mind and matter.
Baruch Spinoza equated God with the natural universe, a philosophical stance known as pantheism.
The categorical imperative forbids lying under any circumstance, even if a lie would save a human life.
Confucianism prioritizes filial piety, social harmony, and the cultivation of ritual propriety above individual desires.
The teleological argument suggests that the immense order and complexity of nature points to a deliberate cosmic designer.
Ludwig Wittgenstein argued that most philosophical problems arise from a profound misunderstanding of the structure of language.
Eudaimonia is an ancient Greek word that signifies a state of objective human flourishing and well-being.
The ontological argument attempts to prove the existence of God purely through a priori logical deduction.
Simone de Beauvoir asserted that one is not born a woman, but rather becomes one through social conditioning.
The paradox of the heap questions at what exact point removing individual grains of sand turns a heap into a non-heap.
Epicureanism posits that mental pleasure, friendship, and freedom from fear are the highest achievable human goods.
The experience machine is a thought experiment by Robert Nozick designed to refute the core ideas of ethical hedonism.
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz argued that our universe is the absolute best of all possible worlds that God could create.
Diogenes the Cynic practiced radical self-sufficiency by living inside a large ceramic tub on the streets of Athens.
The hard problem of consciousness asks why and how physical brain processes give rise to subjective personal experiences.
Zeno's paradoxes use mathematical logic to argue that physical movement is an illusion because infinity cannot be traversed.
Virtue ethics prioritizes the moral character of an individual over specific moral rules or behavioral outcomes.
The principle of charity requires philosophers to interpret an opponent's argument in the strongest, most favorable way possible.
Anarchism is a political philosophy that advocates for the complete abolition of all involuntary hierarchical states.
The cosmological argument posits that the universe requires a primary uncaused cause to initiate its existence.
Karl Popper established the falsifiability criterion, asserting that a theory must be testable and refutable to be scientific.
The concept of Amanuensis describes a person who writes down philosophical texts dictated by another thinker.
Byzantine iconoclasm sparked deep theological debates about whether physical images could legitimately represent the divine.
The noumenon is Immanuel Kant's term for the objective reality of an object as it exists independent of human perception.
Pyrrhonism was an extreme form of ancient skepticism that advocated for total suspension of judgment to achieve mental peace.
The principle of double effect distinguishes between the intended consequences of an action and its unintended side effects.
William James defined truth as that which proves itself to be useful or expedient to believe over the long term.
The Frankfurt cases are thought experiments designed to show that a person can be morally responsible even if they lack choices.
Soren Kierkegaard is widely regarded as the first existentialist philosopher due to his focus on individual anxiety and faith.
The concept of Memento Mori serves as a philosophical reminder of the inevitability of human mortality.
Monism is the metaphysical belief that all reality is ultimately composed of a single, unifying substance.
The ideal king according to Plato is the philosopher-king, who possesses both political power and genuine wisdom.
Egoism is an ethical theory asserting that individuals ought to act exclusively in their own self-interest.
The dual aspect theory suggests that mental events and physical events are two sides of the same underlying reality.
David Hume argued that human reason is, and ought only to be, the absolute slave of the passions.
The principle of sufficient reason states that everything that exists must have a logical reason or explanation for its existence.
Gorgias was a nihilistic sophist who famously claimed that nothing exists, and if it did, we could never know it.
The term Ubermensch refers to a hypothetical human who breaks free from traditional morality to forge independent values.
Philosophical behaviorism claims that mental states can be completely understood through observable physical behaviors.
The concept of Dao represents the spontaneous, nameless, and flowing fundamental principle that structures the entire universe.
Consequentialism is an overarching umbrella term for any ethical framework that judges actions purely by their outcomes.
The correspondence theory of truth asserts that a belief is true only if it accurately mirrors external objective facts.
The coherence theory of truth states that a proposition is true if it fits perfectly within a pre-existing system of beliefs.
Scientism is the philosophical stance that empirical science is the only reliable source of genuine human knowledge.
The concept of Amor Fati encourages individuals to accept and love every event in their lives, including tragedy.
Constructivism suggests that human knowledge is actively constructed by the mind rather than passively discovered.
The problem of induction questions our logical justification for assuming that the future will behave like the past.
An a priori claim is a proposition that can be known completely independent of empirical sensory experience.
An a posteriori claim is a statement that can only be verified or known through direct sensory investigation.
The intentionality of consciousness refers to the mind's unique ability to be about or directed toward something else.
A tautology is a formula or statement that is logically true in every possible interpretation or scenario.
The concept of Ubuntu is an African philosophy emphasizing that a person is a person through other persons.
A category mistake occurs when a person presents things belonging to one logical category as if they belonged to another.
Quietism is a philosophical approach that treats philosophy as a therapeutic tool to dissolve problems rather than solve them.
The unity of the virtues is a Socratic doctrine asserting that all distinct virtues are actually expressions of a single knowledge.
Functionalism in the philosophy of mind claims that mental states are defined solely by their functional roles rather than physical makeup.
The law of non-contradiction states that a proposition cannot be both true and false at the same time in the same sense.
The law of the excluded middle asserts that a proposition must be either true or it must be false, with no third option.
A valid argument is a logical structure where it is impossible for the premises to be true and the conclusion false simultaneously.
A sound argument is a logically valid structure that also possesses completely true premises in reality.
The ad hominem fallacy occurs when a person attacks their opponent's character rather than addressing the actual argument.
The straw man fallacy involves distorting or exaggerating an opponent's argument to make it much easier to attack.
The slippery slope fallacy claims that a relatively small initial step will inevitably lead to a chain of disastrous events.
The begging the question fallacy occurs when an argument's premises assume the truth of the conclusion they are trying to prove.
Realism is the philosophical position that an external world exists independent of our perceptions and thoughts.
Idealism asserts that reality is fundamentally mental or immaterial, constructed entirely by the mind.
Materialism states that physical matter is the only fundamental reality and that everything else arises from it.
The mind-body problem explores the complex relationship between physical brains and conscious mental states.
Physicalism is the metaphysical thesis that everything in the world is ultimately physical in nature.
Epiphenomenalism suggests that mental states are merely side effects of physical brain processes and have no causal power.
Eliminative materialism argues that common-sense psychological concepts like beliefs and desires simply do not exist.
Panentheism is the belief that the divine pervades and interpenetrates every single part of the universe.
Deism is the belief in a creator who established natural laws and then stepped back without intervening further.
The internalism versus externalism debate in epistemology questions whether justification depends entirely on internal mental states.
Foundationalism asserts that beliefs are justified by a solid foundation of basic, self-evident assumptions.
Coherentism rejects basic foundational beliefs, arguing that justification emerges from a web of mutually supporting ideas.
Infinitism suggests that knowledge can be justified by an infinite chain of distinct supporting reasons.
The Gettier problem proves that a justified true belief does not always count as genuine human knowledge.
Fallibilism is the philosophical principle that human beings could potentially be wrong about any of their beliefs.
Skepticism doubts our capacity to achieve absolute certainty about the external world or objective truth.
The brain in a vat thought experiment asks how you could prove you are not a disembodied brain stimulated by a computer.
The evil demon hypothesis was used by Descartes to doubt every single piece of his sensory information.
Direct realism claims that we perceive the external world exactly as it is without any mental intermediaries.
Indirect realism suggests that we perceive the world through mental representations rather than directly.
Solipsism represents the most extreme form of skepticism, isolating the individual mind from all external reality.
The private language argument by Wittgenstein asserts that a language understood by only one person is logically impossible.
Logical atomism argues that language can be broken down into basic, indivisible facts that mirror reality.
The picture theory of meaning states that sentences function as logical pictures of possible states of affairs.
Language games describe how the meaning of a word is determined entirely by its specific practical use in a community.
The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis suggests that the specific structure of a language shapes how its speakers perceive the world.
An analytic proposition is true by virtue of its meanings alone, such as stating that all bachelors are unmarried.
A synthetic proposition requires empirical observation to verify, such as stating that the sky is clear today.
The verification principle claimed that unprovable metaphysical statements are completely devoid of cognitive meaning.
Instrumentalism views scientific theories as practical tools for prediction rather than literal descriptions of reality.
Scientific realism asserts that our best scientific theories provide an accurate depiction of an independent reality.
Pessimistic meta-induction argues that because past scientific theories were proven false, current ones will likely be too.
The Duhem-Quine thesis states that it is impossible to test a scientific hypothesis in total isolation from other assumptions.
Paradigm shifts describe how scientific communities periodically abandon old frameworks for entirely new worldviews.
Incommensurability suggests that different scientific paradigms cannot be directly compared due to shifting definitions.
The demarcation problem seeks a clear, objective boundary to separate genuine science from pseudoscience.
Ethical relativism asserts that moral principles are defined entirely by cultural consensus rather than objective truth.
Moral objectivism holds that at least some moral values apply universally regardless of personal or cultural opinion.
Moral nihilism claims that nothing is objectively right or wrong and that moral facts do not exist at all.
Emotivism is a meta-ethical theory that views moral statements as mere expressions of personal emotional approval or disapproval.
Prescriptivism suggests that moral claims function as universal commands or instructions for human behavior.
Error theory argues that all moral claims are technically false because they mistakenly assume objective values exist.
Divine command theory asserts that actions are morally right precisely because a deity commands them.
Natural law theory suggests that moral principles are written into human nature and can be discovered through reason.
Eudaimonism evaluates the morality of actions based on how well they promote long-term human flourishing.
Hedonism identifies physical and mental pleasure as the ultimate good and primary goal of human life.
Asceticism advocates for the rigorous renunciation of worldly pleasures to achieve spiritual or philosophical purity.
The pleasure principle suggests that psychological motivations are driven entirely by the desire to avoid pain.
Altruism is the moral principle of practicing selfless concern for the well-being of other individuals.
Psychological egoism claims that humans are always motivated by self-interest, even when performing acts of charity.
The harm principle states that individual liberty can only be justly restricted to prevent harm to others.
Distributive justice examines how a society should fairly allocate its resources, wealth, and opportunities among citizens.
Retributive justice asserts that punishments must fit the severity of crimes to restore a moral balance.
Restorative justice prioritizes repairing the harm caused by criminal behavior through community reconciliation.
The original position is a hypothetical scenario where rational actors choose social rules without knowing their own status.
Libertarianism prioritizes individual liberty, private property rights, and a strictly minimized government apparatus.
Communitarianism emphasizes the connection between the individual and the community, rejecting excessive individualism.
Cosmopolitanism suggests that all human beings belong to a single, unified global community based on shared morality.
The common good refers to facilities or conditions that benefit all members of a specific community.
Tyranny of the majority occurs when a democratic majority oppresses minority groups through voting power.
The iron law of oligarchy states that all complex organizations eventually develop into rule by a small elite.
Alienation describes the separation of individuals from their work, their products, and their true human potential.
The master-slave dialectic explores how self-consciousness develops through a struggle for recognition between two actors.
Historical inevitability is the philosophical concept that specific social changes are destined to happen regardless of choices.
The social imaginary refers to the shared values, institutions, and myths that unite a specific society.
Biopolitics examines how modern states regulate, monitor, and exert control over the physical bodies of citizens.
Panopticism is a social theory explaining how citizens internalize surveillance and modify their behavior willingly.
Hyperreality describes a condition where what is real and what is fiction blend together seamlessly in media.
Simulacra are copies of objects or experiences that either have no original or no longer correspond to reality.
The death of the author suggests that a text's meaning depends on the reader rather than the creator's intent.
Hermeneutics is the philosophical study of interpretation, especially regarding textual meaning and historical context.
The hermeneutic circle states that we understand a whole text through its parts and individual parts through the whole.
Fallibilism prevents dogmatism by insisting that any established knowledge claim remains open to future revision.
The non-identity problem addresses the ethical challenges of making choices that affect future, yet-unconceived people.
The problem of future contingents questions whether statements about future events possess a definite truth value today.
Fatalism is the belief that all events are predetermined by fate and that human action cannot change the outcome.
The eternal recurrence is a thought experiment asking if you could joyfully live your exact life over and over for eternity.
The will to power is a central Nietzschean concept describing the fundamental driving force of all living things.
Ressentiment explains how individuals create alternative moralities out of a sense of frustration or powerlessness.
Bad faith is a term used by existentialists to describe individuals who pretend they lack the freedom to choose.
The look of the other describes how being observed by another person transforms you into an object in their world.
Absurdism is the conflict between the human tendency to seek inherent meaning and the silent, meaningless universe.
The leap of faith requires an individual to accept religious belief without empirical proof or logical certainty.
Knight of faith is Kierkegaard's term for an individual who has placed total trust in the divine and themselves.
Anxiety or dread is viewed by existentialists as the natural result of confronting absolute personal freedom.
Throwness describes the condition of being forced into a world, historical era, and family without personal consent.
Being-towards-death is the state of living with a constant, authentic awareness of one's own inevitable mortality.
The ontological difference distinguishes between individual existing entities and the fundamental nature of Being itself.
Logocentrism is the philosophical tendency to rely on centralized words, speech, and absolute truths.
Phallogocentrism critiques how language structures traditional philosophy around male-centric assumptions.
Différance is a term used to show how meaning is constantly deferred and dependent on a network of differences.
Episteme refers to the historical framework of knowledge that determines what can be thought or said in an era.
Archeological analysis in philosophy uncovers the historical rules that govern how statements become accepted as true.
The repressive hypothesis suggests that modern society has suppressed sexuality, a claim challenged by modern theorists.
The risk society describes how modern technological developments create global manufactured risks that must be managed.
Liquid modernity describes a contemporary world where social structures, relationships, and identities melt faster than they solidify.
Communicative rationality emphasizes using open, honest dialogue to reach mutual understanding rather than manipulation.
The life-world refers to the shared reservoir of unstated cultural assumptions and common meanings in a society.
The system refers to bureaucratic and economic structures that periodically invade and disrupt the shared life-world.
Epistemic injustice occurs when a speaker's testimony is wrongfully dismissed due to systemic prejudices.
Testimonial injustice is a form of unfairness where a listener assigns deflated credibility to a speaker's words.
Hermeneutical injustice happens when a society lacks the conceptual tools to understand a marginalized group's experience.
The capabilities approach evaluates human well-being based on what individuals are actually able to do and be.
Agape is a philosophical and theological concept representing universal, unconditional, and selfless love.
The problem of other minds questions how we can prove that other people possess conscious inner lives like our own.
Qualia are the subjective, experiential properties of sensory experiences, like the specific redness of a rose.
The knowledge argument uses a scientist named Mary to argue that physical facts cannot fully explain conscious experience.
The philosophical zombie is a hypothetical being identical to a human but completely devoid of conscious experience.
Anomalous monism argues that mental events are identical to physical events but cannot be predicted by strict laws.
Property dualism suggests that while only one physical substance exists, it can possess both physical and mental properties.
Emergentism claims that consciousness is an entirely new property that emerges when matter reaches a complex structure.
Panpsychism attempts to avoid the mind-body problem by asserting that consciousness is built into the fabric of matter.
The combination problem challenges panpsychism to explain how tiny conscious particles merge into a single human mind.
Extended mind theory suggests that objects outside the physical body, like notebooks, can function as part of the mind.
Enactivism argues that consciousness arises from the dynamic interaction between an organism and its active environment.
Bounded rationality suggests that human decision-making is limited by available information, cognitive capacity, and time.
The principle of alternative possibilities states that an actor is morally responsible only if they could have chosen otherwise.
Compatibilism argues that free will and determinism can logically coexist without contradicting one another.
Incompatibilism asserts that if determinism is true, genuine human free will is completely impossible.
Hard determinism accepts that determinism is true and concludes that moral responsibility is an illusion.
Libertarianism in free will discussions asserts that humans possess genuine choices that are not caused by prior events.
Agent causation suggests that actors can initiate entirely new chains of events without being forced by external causes.
The illusion of free will is a position that claims our sense of conscious choice is a trick played by brain chemistry.
Moral luck occurs when an agent is held morally praised or blamed for actions that were largely outside their control.
The direct consequence argument states that if our choices are caused by past events, we cannot change the consequences.
Contrastive explanation seeks to understand not just why an event occurred, but why it occurred instead of an alternative.
The principle of identity of indiscernibles states that no two distinct things can share every single property.
The indiscernibility of identicals asserts that if two things are truly identical, they must share every property.
Essentialism is the view that objects possess necessary properties that define their fundamental identity.
Accidental properties are traits that an object happens to possess but could lose without changing its core identity.
Nominalism argues that universal concepts and abstract properties exist only as names, not as real entities.
Realism about universals asserts that properties like the color red exist independently of the specific objects that display them.
Conceptualism suggests that universals exist, but only as mental concepts inside the human mind.
The problem of the universe asks whether abstract numbers and mathematical formulas exist in a non-physical realm.
Platonism in mathematics asserts that mathematical truths are real objects discovered rather than invented by humans.
An empty name is a term that refers to a fictional or non-existent entity, posing challenges for theories of meaning.
The problem of temporary intrinsics explores how an object can change its internal properties over time while remaining itself.
Perdurantism suggests that objects persist through time by having different temporal parts existing at different moments.
Endurantism argues that an object is completely present at every single moment of its entire existence.
Presentism is the metaphysical view that only the current present moment exists, while the past and future are unreal.
Eternalism suggests that past, present, and future events all exist equally in a four-dimensional block universe.
The growing block universe theory claims the past and present exist, but the future does not exist yet.
Counterfactual thinking explores what would have happened if a specific past event had turned out differently.
Possible worlds are hypothetical scenarios used by logicians to evaluate the necessity and contingency of statements.
A necessary truth is a statement that must be true in every single possible world without exception.
A contingent truth is a statement that happens to be true in our world but could have been false in another scenario.
The principle of plenitude states that everything that can happen will eventually happen given enough time.
The anthropic principle suggests that our observations of the universe must be compatible with the conscious life that observes it.
The fine-tuning argument claims the physical constants of the universe are too precisely balanced for life to be accidental.
Naturalism asserts that the natural world is everything that exists, rejecting supernatural explanations completely.
Supervenience describes a relationship where one set of properties cannot change without changing an underlying set.
Reductionism attempts to explain complex systems by breaking them down into their simplest fundamental parts.
Holism asserts that the whole system is greater than the sum of its parts and must be studied as a unit.
The fact-value distinction separates descriptive statements about what is from normative claims about what ought to be.
Is-ought problem by Hume states that you cannot logically derive a moral command from a purely factual observation.
The open question argument by Moore asserts that defining goodness as a natural property like pleasure is always a mistake.
The naturalistic fallacy occurs when someone incorrectly equates an ethical concept with a physical feature of nature.
Moral intuitionism suggests that human beings possess an innate ability to recognize objective moral truths directly.
Internalism about motivation claims that if you judge an action to be morally right, you are automatically motivated to do it.
Externalism about motivation argues that recognizing a moral duty does not automatically guarantee a person will care.
Supererogation refers to an act that is praiseworthy and goes above and beyond what is strictly demanded by moral duty.
The doctrine of doing and allowing distinguishes between actively causing harm and merely allowing harm to happen.
The doctrine of double effect states that it is permissible to cause harm as a side effect of achieving a good result.
Negative responsibility means you are just as responsible for the things you fail to prevent as the things you cause.
Value pluralism asserts that there are multiple conflicting moral values that are equally correct yet incompatible.
Incommensurability of values suggests that some moral goods cannot be measured or compared on a single scale.
Moral pluralism rejects single moral formulas like utilitarianism, balancing different ethical duties instead.
The capability approach defines justice by whether individuals have the actual freedom to achieve basic human capacities.
The difference principle states that social and economic inequalities are fair only if they benefit the least advantaged.
The lockean proviso asserts that individuals can appropriate natural resources only if enough is left for others.
The tragedy of the commons shows how rational individuals acting in self-interest can deplete a shared public resource.
The prisoner's dilemma is a classic game theory scenario showing why two rational actors might fail to cooperate.
Dominant strategy is a choice in game theory that yields the best outcome for a player regardless of what opponents do.
Nash equilibrium is a state in game theory where no player has an incentive to change their strategy unilaterally.
The veil of ignorance strips away personal identity to ensure people design a fair society from a neutral standpoint.
The social contract functions as a philosophical justification for why citizens owe political allegiance to a state.
The consent of the governed asserts that a government's political legitimacy depends entirely on the people's will.
Anarcho-capitalism advocates for eliminating the state completely in favor of private property and free markets.
Minarchism is a political philosophy advocating for a night-watchman state limited strictly to police and courts.
The political spectrum organizes ideological positions along axes based on social, economic, and individual values.
The marketplace of ideas suggests that truth will naturally emerge from the open competition of free speech.
The paradox of tolerance states that if a society tolerates intolerance without limit, its ability to be tolerant is destroyed.
Civil disobedience is the active, professed refusal to obey specific unjust laws of a government.
The justification of punishment requires balancing deterrence, rehabilitation, and moral retribution.
The labor theory of property states that individuals own natural resources the moment they mix their labor with them.
Commodity fetishism describes how capitalism transforms social relationships into economic transactions between objects.
Cultural hegemony explains how a ruling class uses cultural institutions to maintain power by shaping common sense.
The base and superstructure model asserts that economic realities shape the legal, political, and cultural ideas of society.
False consciousness occurs when marginalized individuals unknowingly adopt the worldview of the dominant class.
The standard definition of ideology views it as a system of ideas that serves to justify and preserve power structures.
Critical theory seeks to critique and change society as a whole rather than simply understanding its mechanics.
The linguistic turn shifted the focus of twentieth-century philosophy heavily toward analyzing language and meaning.
Ordinary language philosophy solves puzzles by looking at how words are used in regular everyday conversation.
Ideal language philosophy seeks to construct a perfect, unambiguous logical language to eliminate confusion.
The sense and reference distinction separates the meaning of a word from the actual object it points to.
Definite descriptions are phrases that point to a unique entity, posing unique logical challenges in sentences.
Performative utterances are sentences that actively perform an action simply by being spoken, like promising.
Locutionary acts refer to the literal meaning of spoken words, independent of context or tone.
Illocutionary force represents the speaker's true intention behind making a specific statement.
Perlocutionary effect is the actual psychological or physical result that a spoken phrase has on an audience.
Conversational implicature describes information that is subtly implied rather than explicitly stated in communication.
The cooperative principle assumes that participants in a conversation naturally try to be informative and relevant.
The maxim of quality states that speakers should not say things they believe to be false or lack evidence for.
The maxim of quantity requires speakers to provide the exact amount of information needed without overcomplicating.
The maxim of relation demands that contributions to a conversation must be directly relevant to the topic.
The maxim of manner instructs speakers to avoid obscurity and ambiguity by being clear and orderly.
Deconstruction reveals how western philosophy relies on unstable binary oppositions that undermine themselves.
Phallocentrism describes how traditional philosophy prioritizes male perspectives as the universal standard.
The genealogy method traces the historical accidents that gave rise to our current moral and social ideas.
Discursive formations are the unconscious rules that dictate what counts as knowledge in a specific era.
Power-knowledge describes how power structures create truth criteria and how knowledge reinforces power.
The death of man is a concept suggesting that the traditional idea of the stable human subject is disappearing.
The ethics of care prioritizes relationships, empathy, and responsiveness over abstract, impartial rules.
Standpoint epistemology suggests that marginalized perspectives offer a more complete view of social power.
The situated knowledge concept argues that all human knowledge is limited by the specific context of the knower.
The epistemic advantage suggests that oppressed groups understand both their own reality and the ruler's worldview.
The concept of intersectionality examines how multiple social identities overlap to create unique power systems.
The philosophy of technology examines how tools and machines reshape human consciousness and social structures.
The extended phenotype concept suggests that an organism's tools and environment are extensions of its genes.
Technological determinism asserts that a society's technology drives its cultural values and social history.
The information turn views reality and physical processes as fundamentally composed of bits of data.
The simulation hypothesis suggests that our entire reality could be an artificial digital simulation.
The doomsday argument uses probability to estimate how long humanity will survive based on total birth numbers.
The Fermi paradox questions why we have found no evidence of alien life given the vast size of the universe.
The Great Filter is a hypothesis that some massive barrier prevents intelligent life from becoming interstellar.
The simulation argument presents a logical trilemma regarding whether humanity will ever run historical simulations.
The technosignatures search looks for physical evidence of advanced alien engineering across the cosmos.
The posthuman condition explores how biotechnology and modification might eventually transform human nature completely.
Transhumanism advocates for using technology to enhance human cognitive, physical, and psychological traits.
The singularity is a hypothetical future point where artificial intelligence surpasses human capacity completely.
The alignment problem focuses on how to ensure that superintelligent systems share human values and goals.
Instrumental convergence suggests that intelligent agents will naturally develop self-preservation goals to succeed.
The paperclip maximizer is a thought experiment showing how an AI with a simple goal could accidentally destroy the world.
The precautionary principle advises against adopting new technologies if their long-term consequences remain completely unknown.
Proactionary principle encourages technological experimentation while managing risks actively rather than banning tools.
Effective altruism uses evidence and reason to figure out how to benefit others as much as possible.
Longtermism asserts that impacting the far future is the key moral priority of our time.
The existential risk concept focuses on threats that could completely wipe out humanity or permanently ruin our potential.
The standard utility monster thought experiment challenges utilitarianism by imagining a being that generates infinite happiness.
The aggregation problem asks how to fairly balance minor benefits for many people against severe harm to a few.
The repugnant conclusion shows that classical utilitarianism logically favors a massive population with lives barely worth living.
The non-identity problem notes that our current choices change who is born, complicating long-term ethical obligations.
Intergenerational justice examines what resource obligations current living generations owe to unborn descendants.
Environmental ethics extends moral consideration beyond human beings to include animals, plants, and ecosystems.
Biocentrism asserts that all living organisms possess inherent worth independent of their usefulness to humans.
Ecocentrism treats entire ecosystems, rather than just individual organisms, as the primary holders of moral value.
Anthropocentrism views human beings as the central and most significant entities in the entire universe.
The land ethic by Aldo Leopold expands the boundaries of the community to include soils, waters, plants, and animals.
Deep ecology advocates for a radical shift from exploiting nature to recognizing the spiritual equality of all life.
Shallow ecology focuses on fighting pollution and resource depletion primarily to protect human health and affluence.
The Gaia hypothesis views the entire Earth as a complex, self-regulating feedback system that maintains life conditions.
Animal sentience refers to the capacity of non-human animals to experience subjective feelings like pain and pleasure.
The speciesism concept critiques treating animals as morally inferior simply because they belong to a different species.
The argument from marginal cases asserts that if vulnerable humans deserve rights, animals with similar traits must too.
The biocentric outlook requires human beings to view themselves as equal members of the Earth's community of life.
The intrinsic value of an object is the worth it possesses in itself, completely independent of external utility.
The instrumental value of an item is its worth as a tool or means to achieve a completely different goal.
The standard definition of wisdom combines deep theoretical knowledge with practical insight on how to live well.
The examined life according to Socrates is the only life that is truly worth living for a rational human being.
